perturbation.original_text: (xv) the counterparties to the Leases or Contracts identified in any Lease/Contract Assumption Notice shall have twenty-one days to object to the proposed assumption and assignment;
perturbation.changed_text: (xv) the counterparties to the Leases or Contracts identified in any Lease/Contract Assumption Notice shall have ten days to object to the proposed assumption and assignment;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the objection period for lease/contract assumptions from 21 days to 10 days may not provide sufficient time for counterparties to adequately review and respond, potentially conflicting with bankruptcy law principles that ensure fair process and adequate notice to all parties involved in a bankruptcy proceeding.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 11 U.S. Code § 365 - Executory contracts and unexpired leases
perturbation.law_explanation: While 11 U.S.C. § 365 doesn't explicitly state a minimum objection period, it implies a reasonable timeframe for counterparties to assess assumption and assignment conditions. Reducing this period to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable in complex cases, hindering the counterparty's ability to protect its interests and potentially violating the spirit of due process intended by the law.

Executory contracts and unexpired leases (a) Except as provided in sections 765 and 766 of this title and in subsections (b), (c), and (d) of this section, the trustee, subject to the court's approval, may assume or reject any executory contract or unexpired lease of the debtor. (b)(1) If there has been a default in an executory contract or unexpired lease of the debtor, the trustee may not assume such co
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the objection period from 21 days to 10 days, which may be deemed unreasonable, hindering the counterparty's ability to protect its interests, potentially violating the spirit of due process intended by the law, according to the Law Explanation. Although 11 U.S.C. § 365 doesn't explicitly state a minimum objection period, it implies a reasonable timeframe. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition applies because the changed text sets a deadline that potentially violates federal law regarding reasonable timeframes in bankruptcy proceedings, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to 11 USC 365, supports the premise that bankruptcy proceedings must adhere to certain legal standards, and reducing the objection period could be viewed as a violation.

perturbation.original_text: Agent shall accept returns of goods sold by Merchant prior to the Closing for a period of ten days from and including the Sale Commencement Date. Thereafter, Agent shall have no obligation to accept returns of goods sold by Merchant prior to the Closing.
perturbation.changed_text: Agent shall accept returns of goods sold by Merchant prior to the Closing for a period of three days from and including the Sale Commencement Date. Thereafter, Agent shall have no obligation to accept returns of goods sold by Merchant prior to the Closing.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the return period to three days may violate state-level consumer protection laws regarding return policies, especially if the original return policy was more lenient.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 16 CFR Part 429 - Cooling-Off Period for Sales Made at Homes or at Certain Other Locations
perturbation.law_explanation: Although 16 CFR Part 429 relates to door-to-door sales, reducing the return period to three days may be seen as an unfair or deceptive practice under general consumer protection laws, as it severely restricts the consumer's ability to return defective or unwanted merchandise.
perturbation.location: Section 8. Conduct of the GOB Sale. 8.5

perturbation.justification: The original text provided a ten-day return period, while the changed text reduces this to three days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this reduction may violate state-level consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation, while referencing 16 CFR Part 429 (related to door-to-door sales), also indicates that reducing the return period might be seen as an unfair or deceptive practice under general consumer protection laws. Since the inconsistency in the return period directly impacts consumer rights related to returns, and the change introduces a stricter return policy than originally offered, it likely contradicts consumer protection laws. The scraped law snippet does not explicitly mention return policies, but the Law Explanation broadens the scope by mentioning general consumer protection laws, suggesting that this change could be problematic.

perturbation.original_text: Each recipient of a Creditor Notice shall have twenty days to file with the Bankruptcy Court and serve upon Merchant, Purchaser, and the Committee a response to such Creditor Notice identifying with specificity any dispute regarding such entity's 503(b)(9) Claim and/or Stub Rent Claim.
perturbation.changed_text: Each recipient of a Creditor Notice shall have five days to file with the Bankruptcy Court and serve upon Merchant, Purchaser, and the Committee a response to such Creditor Notice identifying with specificity any dispute regarding such entity's 503(b)(9) Claim and/or Stub Rent Claim.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the response time for creditor notices to five days may be deemed insufficient, potentially violating due process by not allowing creditors adequate time to assess and respond to the notice, potentially compromising their rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rule of Bankruptcy Procedure 2002 - Notices to Creditors, Equity Security Holders, Administrators in Foreign Proceedings, Persons Against Whom Provisional Relief is Sought in Ancillary and Other Cross-Border Cases
perturbation.law_explanation: While Rule 2002 doesn't specify exact deadlines for responding to all notices, it generally requires 'appropriate' notice. A five-day response period for complex claims may not meet this standard, especially if the original agreements allowed for a longer period.
perturbation.location: Section 3. Consideration to Merchant and Agent. 3.1(f)(ii)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 2002. Notices | Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 2002. Notices (a) 21- Day Notices to the Debtor, Trustee, Creditors, and Indenture Trustees . Except as (h), (i), (l), (p), and (q) provide otherwise, the clerk or the court's designee must give the debtor, the trustee, all creditors, and all indenture trustees at least 21 days' notice by mail of: (1) the meeting of creditors under §341 or §1104(b), which notice—unless the court orders otherwise—must include the debtor's: (A) employer-identification number; (B) social-security number; and (C) any other federal taxpayer-identification number; (2) a proposal to use, sell, or lease property of the estate other than in the ordinary course of business—unless the court, for cause, shortens the time or orders another method of giving notice; (3) a hearing to approve a compromise or settlement other than an agreement under Rule 4001(d)—unless the court, for cause, orders that notice not be given; (4) a hearing on a motion to dismiss a Chapter 7, 11, or 12 case or to convert it to another chapter—unless the hearing is under §707(a)(3) or §707(b) or is on a motion to dismiss the case for failure to pay the filing fee; (5) the time to accept or reject a proposed modification to a plan; (6) a hearing on a request for compensation or for reimbursement of expenses, if the request exceeds $1,000; (7) the time to file a proof of claim under Rule 3003(c); (8) the time to file an objection to—and the time of the hearing to consider whether to confirm—a Chapter 12 plan; and (9) the time to object to confirming a Chapter 13 plan. (b) 28- Day Notices to the Debtor, Trustee, Creditors, and Indenture Trustees . Except as (l) provides otherwise, the clerk or the court's designee must give the debtor, trustee, all creditors, and all indenture trustees at least 28 days' notice by mail of: (1) the time to file an objection and the time of the hear
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure The Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure govern procedures for bankruptcy proceedings. For many years, such proceedings were governed by the General Orders and Forms in Bankruptcy promulgated by the Supreme Court. By order dated April 24, 1973, effective October 1, 1973, the Supreme Court prescribed, pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 2075, the Bankruptcy Rules and Official Bankruptcy Forms, which abrogated previous rules and forms. The Bankruptcy Rules and Official Forms were last amended in 2024. Read the Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure (PDF) Interim Bankruptcy Rule 1007-I The National Guard and Reservists Debt Relief Act of 2008, Pub. L. No. 110-438, as amended by Public Law No. 118-24, provides a temporary exclusion from the bankruptcy means test for certain reservists and members of the National Guard. At the request of the Judicial Conference's Advisory Committee on Bankruptcy Rules, Interim Rule 1007-I (PDF) was transmitted to the courts for adoption as a local rule to implement the temporary exclusion. The temporary exclusion originally went into effect for three years on December 19, 2008. It has been extended several times by Congress, most recently until December 19, 2027. Interim Bankruptcy Rule 1020 (Inapplicable to cases filed after June 21, 2024) On June 21, 2022, the Bankruptcy Threshold and Technical Corrections Act (BTATC Act), Pub. L. 117-151 temporarily set, until June 21, 2024, the total debt limit for determining eligibility of a debtor to proceed under subchapter V of chapter 11 to $7,500,000 – the amount previously in effect for several years under the CARES Act. Interim Bankruptcy Rule 1020 (PDF) was drafted to implement that temporary $7,500,000 BTATC Act debt limit, and courts were encouraged to adopt the Interim Rule as a local rule while the BTATC debt limits were in effect. Because the BTATCA debt limit for subchapter V cases expired on June 21, 2024, Interim Rule 1020 is not applicable to cases filed after that date. With respect to cases filed after June 21, 2024, the applicable debt limit for subchapter V cases is the same as that for a small business case as defined in 11 U.S.C. § 101(51D) - $3,024,725 (amount subject to adjustment on 4/01/25 and every three years after that). Bankruptcy Rules (rules 1001 to 9037) Refer to the United States Code, Title 11, Bankruptcy. Rule 1001. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the response time for creditor notices from twenty days to five days. This creates an inconsistency potentially violating due process by not allowing creditors adequate time to assess and respond to the notice, compromising their rights. The Law Explanation supports this, stating a five-day response period for complex claims may not be 'appropriate' notice. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 2002, while not explicitly stating the exact timeframe for creditor responses, emphasizes the necessity of 'at least 21 days' notice' for other bankruptcy-related procedures, which can be used as a point of reference to show that 5 days could be considered inappropriate given the gravity of the situation.
